food_subgroup: Mixed fried and baked vegetarian snack platter

serving.natural_language_portion: One shared platter of approximately 400 g, typically served as a starter or snack for 2–4 people, comprising cocktail samosas, french fries, masala fries, onion rings, nachos chips, and garlic mayonnaise dip.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: French Fries (fried), Samosa (Indian snack), Onion Rings (fried), Corn Tortilla Chips (nachos), Mayonnaise
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite platter. Nutrient values estimated by weighted-average composition of the five component foods using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data: French Fries (fried, NDB 21137), Samosa (Indian snack, estimated from ICMR-NIN samosa data), Onion Rings (breaded and fried, NDB 21140), Corn Tortilla Chips (NDB 19056), and Mayonnaise (NDB 04025). Approximate component weight fractions: samosas 20%, french fries 20%, masala fries 20%, onion rings 20%, nachos 15%, garlic mayo 5%. Masala fries treated as spiced french fries with negligible macro difference. Garlic mayo treated as standard mayonnaise. Values are estimates; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fried potato products and corn chips — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for French Fries (GI ~63), Corn Chips (GI ~63), and Samosa (GI ~55 estimated from pastry/potato composite)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite platter. GI estimated as weighted average of component GI values sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database: French Fries GI ~63 (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008), Corn Chips GI ~63 (University of Sydney GI Database), Onion Rings GI ~65 (estimated from breaded fried starch analogue), Samosa GI ~55 (estimated from potato-pastry composite). Weighted average across components yields GI ~65, classified as medium (56–69). Glycemic load per serving = (65 × 43.5 × 400) / (100 × 100) = 113.1 g GL for the full 400 g platter.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1520 mg per platter serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this platter due to its high sodium load from seasoned fries, nachos, and garlic mayonnaise, which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake and elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate content and medium-to-high glycemic index (~65) with a very high glycemic load per serving (~113 g GL)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this platter because its high refined carbohydrate content and medium GI produce a very high glycemic load per serving, likely causing significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (22 g/100 g), saturated fat (3.8 g/100 g), trans fat (0.2 g/100 g), and sodium from deep-fried components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this platter due to its combination of high total fat, saturated fat, trans fat from deep-frying, and high sodium, all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veg Platter is a popular Indian-origin vegetarian sharing platter served at casual dining and takeaway restaurants in Singapore, including at establishments such as #Foodcoholic. The platter comprises five distinct fried and baked snack components: cocktail samosas (small triangular pastry parcels filled with spiced potato and peas), French fries (deep-fried potato strips), masala fries (French fries seasoned with Indian spice blends such as chaat masala or cumin), onion rings (battered and deep-fried sliced onions), and nachos chips (corn tortilla chips). These are served alongside a garlic mayonnaise dipping sauce. The overall flavour profile is savoury, spiced, and rich, with crispy textures dominating. Nutritionally, the platter is energy-dense (~390 kcal per 100 g) and high in refined carbohydrates (~43.5 g/100 g) and fat (~22 g/100 g), with a medium glycemic index (~65) but a very high glycemic load per full serving (~113 g GL for 400 g). Sodium content is high (~380 mg/100 g), making the platter a concern for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The platter is vegetarian but not vegan due to the egg-containing mayonnaise. It contains gluten from the samosa pastry, onion ring batter, and nachos. This dish is commonly ordered as a starter or party snack and is well suited for group sharing.
common_user_queries: Samosa fries onion rings platter calories

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite platter. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of component foods using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and ICMR-NIN data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for the closest matching component foods. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and documented, but direct measurement data for this specific platter are not available.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and ICMR-NIN component data; no single approved-source entry exists for this exact platter. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a fully plant-based and egg-free platter with no animal-derived ingredients except mayonnaise, which contains negligible B12; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the component foods. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): deep-fried plant-based snack components contain no meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~65, medium) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for French Fries, Corn Chips, and analogous pastry-potato composites; no direct GI measurement exists for this platter. 5. The cuisine_tag 'Chicken Dish' in the input record is incorrect for this vegetarian platter and has been disregarded. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are approximate. 7. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate for a shared platter for 2–4 people based on typical restaurant portion sizes; no official serving size is defined for this product.
